nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or standing water: rice paddies, roadside ditches, ponds, swamps, and heavily vegetated floodplain pools, often in lowland areas of the Irrawaddy drainage basin

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, floating plants such as frogbit or water lettuce)
shaded areas and surface cover for bubble-nest building
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin release and visual breaks
open swimming areas alongside planted refuges
gentle filtration to avoid disturbing the labyrinth organ and bubble nest

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods: high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and small granules form the staple diet. Relishes live or frozen small invertebrates — mosquito larvae, daphnia, brine shrimp, micro-worms, and bloodworms. Algae-based flake and blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini) round out the diet. In the wild, feeds on zooplankton, small insects, and plant material at the surface and mid-water.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (e.g., neon tetra, ember tetra)
small rasboras (e.g., harlequin rasbora, chili rasbora)
avoidWith: Banded Gourami (Trichogaster fasciata, fw-fish-148) — hybridization risk in captivity
other male dwarf gouramis in small tanks (male-male aggression during spawning)
boisterous or nippy tankmates that will stress the gourami

summary: A peaceful, labyrinth-breathing gourami from Myanmar’s Irrawaddy basin, recognized by its characteristically thick, fleshy lips. Easy to keep and breed; males build bubble nests. Three selectively bred trade forms exist: Standard, Sunset (orange), and Red. Hybridization risk with Banded Gourami (T. fasciata) in captivity.
careNotes: The Thick-lipped Gourami (Trichogaster labiosa, formerly Colisa labiosa) is an undemanding labyrinth fish from the slow-moving, often oxygen-depleted waters of Myanmar’s Irrawaddy drainage. Like all labyrinth fish it must have access to warm, humid air at the surface — a tight-fitting lid that traps warmth is important, and cold draughts can injure the labyrinth organ. A tank of 60 L with dense planting, floating cover, and gentle filtration suits a pair well; larger volumes allow more comfortable community keeping. Males display richer coloration and are slightly smaller and more slender than females, with the characteristic thickened lips pronounced in both sexes. Three distinct trade color forms exist: the natural Standard form, the orange Sunset form, and an intensely colored Red form — all three are selectively bred morphs of the same species. Males become mildly territorial during spawning but are generally peaceful outside breeding season. Avoid housing with Banded Gourami (T. fasciata, fw-fish-148) as hybridization is documented in captivity and hybrids can be fertile, compromising both species in the trade. Maintain soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water replicating the Irrawaddy lowlands; the species tolerates a range of water conditions but thrives when parameters are stable. Perform weekly partial water changes of 25–30% to maintain water quality. Avoid strong current; sponge or low-flow hang-on-back filtration is ideal.
breedingNotes: Typical bubble-nest builder. Condition the pair on live or frozen foods. The male constructs a floating bubble nest, often anchored to floating plants or the surface film near the edge of the tank. Spawning is preceded by a circling courtship display. The male wraps around the female in an embrace, fertilizing eggs as they are released; eggs float up into the nest. Clutches of 100–800 eggs are common. Remove the female after spawning as the male guards the nest aggressively. Fry hatch in 24–48 hours and become free-swimming in a further 2–3 days. First foods: infusoria, commercial fry food, or fresh-hatched brine shrimp nauplii; progress to micro-worms and crushed flake. Remove the male once fry are free-swimming. Breeding difficulty is rated easy compared to other gouramis.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Trichogaster labiosa
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Irrawaddy drainage, Myanmar), adult size, temperature range, pH, hardness, diet, breeding behavior, and care notes. Formerly placed in genus Colisa; current accepted name Trichogaster labiosa confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Trichogaster labiosa
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), adult size (to 9 cm), native range (Myanmar, Irrawaddy basin), temperature (22–28°C), and synonym Colisa labiosa. Confirmed labyrinth organ and bubble-nest breeding.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Gourami Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care perspective: confirmed easy care level, omnivore diet taking flake/pellets/live foods, peaceful temperament, and small tank suitability (55+ L). Noted labyrinth fish surface-air access requirement and bubble-nest breeding behavior.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Thick-lipped Gourami
additional.notes: UK trade perspective; confirmed three color forms (Standard, Sunset/orange, Red), Myanmar origin (Irrawaddy), peaceful community suitability, and hybridization risk with Banded Gourami (T. fasciata). Noted typical adult size 7–9 cm and pH 6.0–7.5.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Labyrinth Fish of Southeast Asia
additional.notes: Background reference on Osphronemidae taxonomy, labyrinth organ physiology, and species complex within Trichogaster/Colisa group. Confirmed hybridization risk between T. labiosa and T. fasciata in captivity and trade confusion between the two species.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder. Male constructs floating nest near surface cover and guards eggs and fry. Clutch size 100–800 eggs; hatch in 24–48 h; fry free-swimming in 2–3 days. First foods: infusoria or brine shrimp nauplii. Remove female after spawning; remove male once fry are free-swimming.
